desc: LA's largest grasshopper — up to 2.5 in. Gray-green, strong flier. Forms loose swarms. Desert scrub to urban gardens.

fm.Color: Gray-green; dark dorsal stripe; yellow-green when young
fm.Behavior: Strong flier; forms loose groups; can be crop pest
fm.Habitat: Desert, chaparral, gardens — any open area

desc: Named for crop devastation. Medium brown grasshopper. One of the most abundant LA County grasshoppers.

fm.Color: Brown with dark stripe; pale lateral stripe
fm.Behavior: Very common; can reach outbreak densities

desc: Band-winged grasshopper that flashes pale yellow hindwings in flight, then vanishes on landing. Master of crypsis on bare ground.

fm.Color: Gray-brown (cryptic on bare ground); pale yellow HW visible in flight
fm.Behavior: Crepitating flight (crackly sound); flash-and-vanish display
fm.Habitat: Bare ground — trails, roadsides, rocky areas

desc: Band-winged grasshopper with striking red hindwings flashed in display flights. Dark body.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; bright red hindwings visible in flight
fm.Behavior: Crepitating display flight; red wing flash; cryptic at rest

desc: Large band-winged grasshopper. Black hindwings with pale border — conspicuous hovering display. Common on bare ground.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; black HW with pale yellow border (diagnostic in flight)
fm.Behavior: Males hover in display flight showing black wings; loud crepitation
fm.Habitat: Bare ground, roadsides, parking lots, dry paths

desc: Large yellow-green grasshopper with distinctive chevron pattern on hind femora. Important crop pest.

fm.Color: Yellow-green; black chevron marks on hind femora (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Strong flier; crop pest in outbreak years

desc: Elongated, slender — resembles a grass blade. Green. Associated with marsh grasses.

fm.Color: Green with pale lateral stripe; extremely slender
fm.Behavior: Sits vertically on grass stems; very cryptic

desc: Small, silvery grasshopper found exclusively on creosote bush. Cryptic coloration matches host perfectly.

fm.Behavior: Sits motionless on creosote; very cryptic; specialist
fm.Habitat: Desert creosote scrub — obligate on Larrea

desc: Green katydid common in gardens and brushy areas. Males sing at night. Named for forked cerci.

fm.Sound: Males sing at night — rapid series of lisps
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal singer; feeds on broadleaf foliage

desc: Large, green, broad-winged katydid. Looks like a leaf. Males tick from treetops at night. Eggs laid in rows on twigs.

fm.Sound: Males produce sharp ticks/clicks at night
fm.Behavior: Arboreal; nocturnal; excellent leaf mimic

desc: Similar to M. retinerve but broader wings with more angular shape. Common garden leaf mimic.

fm.Behavior: Arboreal; nocturnal; superb leaf mimic

desc: Pale green, delicate cricket whose males produce a pure-toned trill that's the quintessential summer night sound. Temperature-dependent chirp rate.

fm.Color: Pale green; translucent wings; slender; delicate
fm.Sound: Pure musical trill; chirp rate = temperature (Dolbear's Law)
fm.Behavior: Males trill from leaf edges; chew feeding holes to amplify sound
fm.Habitat: Gardens, orchards, hedges — on broadleaf foliage

desc: The classic chirping cricket of warm evenings. Introduced. Now also commercially raised as food/pet food.

fm.Sound: Familiar chirping — males rub wings together
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to warmth and lights; enters buildings

desc: Bizarre burrowing cricket with mole-like forelegs. Burrows through soil, damaging turf. Rarely seen above ground.

fm.Color: Brown; velvety; enlarged front legs for digging
fm.Sound: Males produce churring song from burrow entrance at night
fm.Behavior: Subterranean; mole-like forelegs dig through soil; flies to lights

desc: Introduced from India. Parthenogenetic (all females). Bright green stick insect on ivy and ornamental plants.

fm.Behavior: Parthenogenetic (no males); slow-moving; excellent crypsis; drops to ground when disturbed
fm.Habitat: Urban gardens — ivy, privet, roses, ornamentals

desc: Native stick insect. Brown, shorter antennae than phasmids. Chaparral and coastal sage scrub.

fm.Color: Brown to gray-brown; twig-like; short antennae
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hangs motionless on chaparral branches by day

desc: LA's native mantis. Green or brown. Smaller than introduced species. Males fly to lights at night.

fm.Color: Green or brown (dimorphic); wings shorter than abdomen in ♀
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; males fly to lights; cannibalistic mating
fm.Diet: Predator — any insect it can catch

desc: Introduced. The classic 'praying mantis.' Larger than native species. Green with black spot inside forelegs (diagnostic).

fm.Color: Green (rarely brown); black eyespot on inner foreleg (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; sold commercially for biocontrol
fm.Season: May–Nov; egg cases on stems overwinter

desc: Introduced. Largest mantis in LA — up to 4 in. Tan-green with distinctive green lateral stripe on wings.

fm.Color: Tan to green; green lateral wing stripe
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; catches hummingbirds at feeders occasionally
fm.Diet: Predator — large insects, occasionally small vertebrates
fm.Season: May–Nov; large egg cases on branches

desc: Desert mantis similar to S. californica but typically found in drier habitats at desert edge.

fm.Color: Green or brown; often more brownish than S. californica
fm.Behavior: Ambush predator; desert-adapted

desc: Tiny native mantis — easily overlooked. Gray-brown, ground-dwelling. LA's smallest mantis.

fm.Behavior: Ground-dwelling; runs rather than flies; easily missed due to size
fm.Habitat: Rocky ground, sandy soil, desert scrub

desc: Small, bright green katydid. Introduced. Very common in urban gardens. Sings at night.

fm.Behavior: Common garden katydid; nocturnal singer

desc: Major crop pest across western NA. Can form loose swarms during outbreaks. Brown with red hind tibiae.

fm.Description: Major crop pest across western NA

desc: Large green grasshopper to 2.5 in. Related to desert locust. Feeds on many plants.
Large bright green grasshopper of desert margins. Strong flier. On creosote and mesquite.

fm.Habitat: Large bright green grasshopper of desert margins

desc: Spectacular black, red, yellow, and blue grasshopper. Flightless — relies on warning coloration. Desert margins.
Common garden earwig with rear pincers. Nocturnal omnivore. Female guards eggs — rare maternal care in insects.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal omnivore; ♀ guards eggs

desc: Brown grasshopper of grasslands and agriculture. Can form dense populations in dry years.

fm.Description: Brown grasshopper of grasslands and agriculture

desc: Pale green tree cricket. Chirp rate correlates with temperature (Dolbear's Law). The thermometer cricket.
Large band-winged grasshopper with three dark forewing bands. Displays yellow hindwings in flight.

fm.Description: Large band-winged grasshopper with three dark forewing bands

desc: Gray-brown grasshopper with bright coral-red hind tibiae. Early spring species of open ground.

fm.Description: Gray-brown grasshopper with bright coral-red hind tibiae

desc: Bright green katydid resembling a leaf. Nocturnal stridulation. Found in oaks and garden trees.
Large bald-headed cricket. Nocturnal burrower. Powerful jaws. Called niña de la tierra. Drumming courtship.

fm.Behavior: Powerful jaws; drumming courtship

desc: Robust flightless katydid of chaparral. Males stridulate at night. Omnivorous — eats plants and insects.
Black cricket with familiar chirping song. Temperature affects chirp rate. Important food for many predators.
Small wingless stick insect on Ceanothus. Classic subject of speciation research. Some populations parthenogenetic.
Humpbacked wingless cricket in dark spaces — garages, basements, under rocks. Long antennae. Nocturnal.

fm.Behavior: Wingless; very long antennae; jumps when disturbed

desc: Small brown cockroach replacing oriental cockroach in SoCal. Males can fly. Prefers outdoors.

fm.Behavior: Replacing oriental cockroach in SoCal

desc: Small tan cockroach — world's most common indoor pest species. Rapid reproduction. Exclusively indoor.

fm.Color: Tan with two dark pronotal stripes
fm.Behavior: Rapid reproduction; nocturnal

desc: Large reddish-brown cockroach to 1.5 in. Despite name, of African origin. Called waterbug. Can fly.
Silvery wingless fish-shaped insect in bathrooms and closets. Ancient lineage — predates dinosaurs by 100 million years. Eats paper, glue, starch.

fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; very fast; eats paper and starch

desc: Similar to silverfish but mottled gray-brown and heat-loving. Found near water heaters, ovens, furnaces. Prefers >90°F.

fm.Habitat: Near heat sources — water heaters, ovens
fm.Behavior: Heat-seeking; prefers >90°F

desc: Large shiny dark cockroach called 'waterbug.' Prefers cool damp areas. Being displaced by Turkestan cockroach in SoCal.

fm.Behavior: Slow runner; strong musty smell; declining

desc: Distinctive insect with elongated 'neck' raised cobra-like. A living fossil — unchanged since the Jurassic. Predatory. California endemic genus.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black; elongated prothorax; clear wings
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — on bark of live oaks
fm.Behavior: Raises front body like a snake; predatory

desc: The most commonly encountered mantis in LA gardens. Smaller than Chinese Mantis. Distinctive purple-blue eyespot on inner hindwing. Introduced from Mediterranean.

fm.Color: Green or brown; purple-blue eyespot on inner HW
fm.Habitat: Gardens, shrubs — the common LA garden mantis

desc: Dark wingless earwig with banded yellow-brown legs. Tropical origin. Common in gardens, greenhouses, under pots. More heat-tolerant than European Earwig.

fm.Season: Year-round; more tropical than European Earwig

desc: Humpbacked wingless cricket in garages, basements, and crawl spaces. Extremely long antennae. Introduced from Asia. Cannot chirp. Jumps erratically when disturbed.

fm.Size: ½–¾ in body; antennae much longer
fm.Habitat: Garages, basements, crawl spaces — dark moist areas
fm.Behavior: Cannot chirp; jumps erratically when startled

desc: Very large bright green katydid. Loud buzzing song audible over 200m. Introduced from Europe. Males sing from treetops. Can bite but not venomous.

fm.Habitat: Trees, tall vegetation — sings from canopy
fm.Song: Very loud continuous buzz; audible at distance

desc: Tiny ground-dwelling grasshopper with pronotum extending past wing tips. Found on bare moist soil. Semi-aquatic — can swim. Often overlooked due to small size.

fm.Habitat: Moist bare soil, pond edges — semi-aquatic
fm.Behavior: Can swim; pronotum extends beyond wings

desc: Tiny elongated insect that spins silk from enlarged front tarsi — unique among insects. Lives in silk galleries on tree bark and walls. Colonial. Often overlooked.

fm.Color: Dark brown; soft-bodied; enlarged front feet
fm.Habitat: Tree bark, walls — colonial silk galleries
fm.Behavior: Spins silk from front feet (unique); lives in galleries

desc: Spectacular large grasshopper — black with yellow stripes, orange-red wings. Desert species. Toxic — aposematic coloring warns predators.

fm.Warning: Toxic — produces foul secretion from thorax

desc: Tiny cricket-like insect with huge hind legs adapted for jumping on sand. Desert specialist. Nocturnal. Rarely seen.
Small indoor cockroach preferring dry warm locations (unlike German cockroach). Brown with two light bands across wings. Found in bedrooms, living rooms.

fm.Color: Light brown; two pale transverse bands

desc: Medium grasshopper with transparent hindwings. Pest of rangeland during outbreaks. Gregarious — can form dense swarms.
Tropical house cricket — increasingly replacing Acheta domesticus in pet trade and escaped populations. Smaller, tan with dark crossbands. Chirps at night. Originally from SW Asia.
Native western earwig — unlike the introduced European earwig, this species has toothed forceps (hence 'dentata'). Found under bark and rocks in foothill habitats.
Invasive South African mantis established in SoCal since ~2014. Smaller and greener than native mantis species. Displacing native Stagmomantis. Females cannibalistic during mating.

fm.Color: Bright green; smaller than native mantises

desc: Trailside grasshopper — common along chaparral and sage scrub trails. Gray-brown with distinctive hump-backed profile. Band-winged — pale blue hindwings flash in flight.

fm.Color: Gray-brown; humped profile; pale blue HW in flight

desc: Mexican bush katydid — bright green leaf-mimic. Common in coastal sage scrub and gardens. Sings a rapid series of soft ticks at night. Broad flat wings perfectly mimic leaves.

fm.Color: Bright green; broad flat leaf-shaped wings

desc: California chaparral katydid — endemic to California chaparral. Flattened body with brown coloring matches dead leaves. Unusual wing-clapping courtship display.
Western brush cricket — small brown cricket of chaparral and sage scrub understory. Nocturnal. High-pitched continuous trill at night. Common but rarely seen.
Mahogany Jerusalem cricket — large bald-headed insect with mahogany-colored body. Nocturnal burrower. Powerful jaws. Drumming communication through soil vibrations.

fm.Behavior: Drums abdomen on soil for communication

desc: Yarrow's grasshopper — medium spur-throated grasshopper of mountain chaparral and scrub. Brownish with dark femur bars. Common in San Gabriel Mountains.
Fontana grasshopper — band-winged grasshopper of rocky desert washes. Cryptic on pale substrate. Blue-tinted hindwings flash during flight then vanish on landing.

fm.Color: Pale gray-tan; blue-tinted HW in flight

desc: Shield-backed katydid of chaparral. Wingless. Large and robust. Nocturnal — found crossing trails at night. Omnivorous. Can deliver painful defensive bite.
Texan spot-winged grasshopper — small grasshopper of dry grassland and scrub. Distinctive dark spot on hindwing. Crepitating (crackling) flight display.
Ashy spur-throat grasshopper — gray-brown grasshopper of chaparral and sage scrub. Common on trails. Part of the very species-rich Melanoplus genus.
Pacific green grasshopper — bright green grasshopper of moist grassland and riparian areas. One of the few all-green grasshoppers in LA County. Common in irrigated lawns.
Dragon lubber grasshopper — large chaparral grasshopper with bizarre elongated head and spiny pronotum. Wingless. California endemic. Looks prehistoric.
Wheeler's mantis — native praying mantis of desert scrub. Smaller and more slender than Chinese mantis. Tan-brown coloring. Declining in areas colonized by non-native mantises.
Intermediate-winged grasshopper — large band-winged grasshopper of desert and foothill grassland. Bright yellow hindwings with dark band. Crackling flight display.

fm.Color: Brown; yellow HW with dark band in flight

desc: Ramona band-winged grasshopper — medium grasshopper of open foothill habitat. Red or orange hindwings flash during flight display, then vanish on landing.

fm.Color: Brown mottled; red/orange HW in flight

desc: California shield-backed katydid — dark wingless katydid of coastal sage scrub. Nocturnal. Omnivorous scavenger. Can deliver painful bite. California native.
Chaparral shield-backed katydid — large wingless katydid found under rocks and bark in chaparral. Nocturnal predator/scavenger. California endemic.
Cream grasshopper — small pale grasshopper of desert washes. Excellent camouflage on sandy substrate. Band-winged — pale hindwings visible in flight.
Shore band-winged grasshopper — coastal grasshopper with pale blue hindwings. Found on sandy beaches and dunes. Excellent camouflage on sand.

fm.Color: Sand-colored; pale blue HW in flight

desc: invertebrate. Maritime Earwig. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. White-lined Grasshopper. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. Strenuous Grasshopper. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. Shore Earwig. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. Western Sagebrush Grasshopper. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. tenuipennis. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. Snakeweed Grasshopper. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. Pacific Bush Grasshopper. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. Santa Monica Grasshopper. various habitats. Regularly recorded in LA County.
invertebrate. California Thread-leg Katydid. various habitats.
invertebrate. Prairie Tree Cricket. various habitats.
invertebrate. Pacific Ground Mantis. various habitats.
invertebrate. bifasciatus. various habitats.
invertebrate. California Rose-winged Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Caerulean-winged Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Southern Coast Bush Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Riley's Tree Cricket. various habitats.
invertebrate. Graceful Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Groove-headed grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. West Coast Ant Cricket. various habitats.
invertebrate. Aztec Pygmy Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Saussure's Desert Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Chestnut Shieldback. various habitats.
invertebrate. robustus. various habitats.
invertebrate. Ball's Little Shieldback. various habitats.
invertebrate. Egyptian Bird Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Sierran White-whiskered Grasshopper. various habitats.
invertebrate. Four-spotted Tree Cricket. various habitats.
invertebrate. phryneicus. various habitats.
invertebrate. Variable Field Cricket. various habitats.
invertebrate. Morse's Shieldback. various habitats.
invertebrate. Shasta Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 9 observations.
invertebrate. Desert Clicker Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 9 observations.
invertebrate. Broad-tipped Conehead. Recorded in LA County with 9 observations.
invertebrate. San Diego Camel Cricket. Recorded in LA County with 9 observations.
invertebrate. Bruner Longwing. Recorded in LA County with 9 observations.
invertebrate. Ovate Shieldback. Recorded in LA County with 9 observations.
invertebrate. Santa Monica Mountains Shieldback. Recorded in LA County with 8 observations.
invertebrate. Timberlake's Desert Long-horned Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 8 observations.
invertebrate. Spotted Bird Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 7 observations.
invertebrate. Striped Slant-faced Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 7 observations.
invertebrate. Mexican Pygmy Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 7 observations.
invertebrate. Red-lined Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 6 observations.
invertebrate. Olancha Red-wing Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 6 observations.
invertebrate. Riley's Short-wing Grasshopper. Recorded in LA County with 5 observations.
invertebrate. Vocal Field Cricket. Recorded in LA County with 5 observations.
invertebrate. Coastal Shieldback. Recorded in LA County with 5 observations.
